---
title: "Week 8: Shiny"
author: "Joe Nese"
institute: "University of Oregon<br>Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: default
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 3.5
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
execute: 
  echo: true
  warning: false
embed-resources: true
---

```{r}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(tictoc)
library(gt)

table_style <- function(data) {
  data %>%
    opt_table_lines(extent = "none") %>%
    opt_table_outline(style = "solid", width = px(3), color = "black") %>%
    tab_options(
      heading.border.bottom.color = "black",
      heading.border.bottom.style = "solid",
      table.border.top.color = "black",
      table_body.hlines.color = "black",
      table_body.border.top.color = "black",
      table_body.border.top.style = "solid",
      table_body.border.bottom.color = "black",
      table_body.border.bottom.style = "solid",
      column_labels.border.bottom.color = "black",
      column_labels.border.bottom.style = "solid",
      column_labels.border.top.color = "black",
      column_labels.background.color = "#009E73",
      table.width = pct(100),
      container.width = pct(100)
    )
}

theme_set(theme_minimal(20))

update_geom_defaults("point", list(size = 3))
```

# Shiny

**Week 8**

## Agenda {.smaller}

* Review Lab 3
* What is Shiny?
    + first shiny app
    + UI & Server components
    + Inputs & Outputs
    + Layouts
* Introduce reactivity

**Learning objectives**

* Be able to create basic interactive plots with Shiny
* Understand basic layouts
* Understand UI / Server separation
* Have a basic understanding of reactivity

# Review Lab 3

# Getting Started with Shiny

Creating your first Shiny app

## The basics!

Do the following:

* Create a new `R` project
* Select "*Shiny Web App*"
* Select the directory
    + use "*Single File (app.R)*"
    + rather than "*Multiple File (ui.R/server.R split)*"
* Click "*Create*"

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

## First thing - Run App!

* I like to open in the browser
* What's going on here? Talk with your neighbor and see if you can deduce the basic functionality

`r countdown::countdown(minutes = 2)`

# UI & Server

The two halves of every Shiny app

## Shiny app anatomy {.smaller}

1. UI (user interface)
    + what the user sees and interacts with
2. Server
    + the `R` code that runs things
    
. . .

- The two communicate through named `inputs` and `outputs`
- `shinyApp(ui, server)` wires them together and launches the app
- The app is always named `app.R`

## `ui` 

* The `ui` defines the look and feel of the app
* Defines `inputs`  - widgets the user controls
    + for the `server`, where functions are actually evaluated
* Declares **where** `outputs` will be rendered
* Built from nested `R` functions that generate HTML


## `ui` {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

* Every input needs a unique `inputId`
* Here we have defined a `sliderInput`, which we're calling `bins`
    + The `label` is what will be displayed
    + It will take on values from `1` to `50`, and will start at `30` by default
* Access the specific value the user selects within the `server` through `input$inputId`
    - `input$bins`

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|10|11|12|13|14|15"
# Define UI for application that draws a histogram
ui <- fluidPage(

    # Application title
    titlePanel("Old Faithful Geyser Data"),

    # Sidebar with a slider input for number of bins 
    sidebarLayout(
        sidebarPanel(
            sliderInput(
                        inputId = "bins",
                        label = "Number of bins:",
                        min = 1,
                        max = 50,
                        value = 30)
        ),

        # Show a plot of the generated distribution
        mainPanel(
           plotOutput("distPlot")
        )
    )
)

```


:::

::::

## Inputs {.smaller}

`input` is a basically a list object that contains objects from the `ui`

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
ui <- fluidPage(
  ...
  sliderInput(
                        inputId = "bins",
                        label = "Number of bins:",
                        min = 1,
                        max = 50,
                        value = 30)
        )
  ...
)
```

After writing this code, `input$bins` will be a available in the server, and the value it takes will depend on the browser input (starting at 30)

## `server()` {.smaller}

* A **function** with three arguments
    + `input` — read-only list of values from the UI
    + `output` — list you assign rendered objects to
    + `session` — metadata about the user session (advanced) which we won't cover
    
## `server()` {.smaller}

* A **function** with three arguments
    + `input` — read-only list of values from the UI
    + `output` — list you assign rendered objects to
    + ~~`session` — metadata about the user session (advanced) which we won't cover~~

## `server()` {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
* A **function** with three arguments
    + `input` — read-only **list** of values from the UI
    + `output` — **list** you assign rendered objects to
* The `server()` **function** takes the input from the `ui` and puts it in normal `R` code
* Use `render*()` functions to create outputs:
    + `renderPlot()` *from*  `plotOutput()`
    + `renderText()` *from* `textOutput()`
    + `renderTable()`*from* `tableOutput()`
* The name of render must match the name in the UI
* The input object is passed to the `server` function

:::

## `server()` {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

* Here, we're creating one output object, called `distPlot`
    + The result is then called through the `ui` (line 30 shiny template)
* Use `render*()` functions to create outputs:
    + Here, `renderPlot()`
* The name of render must match the name in the UI
    + Here, `distPlot`
    + `output$distPlot`
    + from the `ui` in `plotOutput("distPlot")`
* The input object is passed to the `server` function
    + access the web page’s user input fields like you’re grabbing a value from a list or data frame
    + actually reading a reactive value
    + `input$inputId`
    + Here, `input$bins`

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|4|7"
# Define server logic required to draw a histogram
server <- function(input, output) {

    output$distPlot <- renderPlot({
        # generate bins based on input$bins from ui.R
        x    <- faithful[, 2]
        bins <- seq(min(x), max(x), length.out = input$bins + 1)

        # draw the histogram with the specified number of bins
        hist(x, breaks = bins, col = 'darkgray', border = 'white',
             xlab = 'Waiting time to next eruption (in mins)',
             main = 'Histogram of waiting times')
    })
}

```

:::

::::

## Let's try something

Remove lines 39-46, and replace it with `print(input$bins)`

Run the app

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "5"
# Define server logic required to draw a histogram
server <- function(input, output) {

    output$distPlot <- renderPlot({
      print(input$bins)
    })
}
```

. . .

Feels like it should work, but it doesn't

Any ideas why?

## Text output {.smaller}

Not everything is a plot — Shiny handles text, tables, images, and more

With `print(input$bins)` we are no longer rendering a plot, we are rendering text

The `render*__()` / `*__Output()` pair must always match

Trying to `print()` in the server won't work outside a reactive context

. . .

**Fix it**

Change

- `renderPlot` to `renderText` 
- `plotOutput` to `textOutput`

Does it work now?

## Input / Output Pairings

```{r}
#| echo: false
pairings <- tibble::tribble(
           ~"UI Output Function", ~"server",                        ~"Use Case",
          "`plotOutput(\"id\")`",            "`renderPlot({ … })`",           "`{ggplot2}` / base plots",
         "`tableOutput(\"id\")`",           "`renderTable({ … })`",             "Simple HTML tables",
     "`DT::DTOutput(\"id\")`",       "`DT::renderDT({ … })`",        "Interactive Tables (`{DT}`)",
     "`reactable::reactableOutput(\"id\")`",       "`reactable::renderReactable({ … })`", "Interactive Tables (`{reactable}`)",
          "`textOutput(\"id\")`",            "`renderText({ … })`",           "Plain text / strings",
  "`verbatimTextOutput(\"id\")`",           "`renderPrint({ … })`",           "Console-style output",
            "`uiOutput(\"id\")`",              "`renderUi({ … })`",            "Dynamic UI elements",
         "`imageOutput(\"id\")`",           "`renderImage({ … })`",                         "Images"
  )

pairings |> 
  gt::gt() |> 
  table_style() |> 
  cols_label(
    server = md("Server `render*()` Function"),
  ) |> 
  fmt_markdown(columns = everything()) |> 
  opt_row_striping()
```


## Challenge {.smaller}

* Try producing essentially the same thing as the default, but use `ggplot` instead of base plotting
* Reminder, the data are called `faithful`, and you'll be showing the distribution of `waiting`
* Be sure to look at **ALL** of the code within the `server`

`r countdown::countdown(minutes = 10)`

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

. . .


```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|6|9-15"
# Define server logic required to draw a histogram
server <- function(input, output) {

    output$distPlot <- renderPlot({
        # generate bins based on input$bins from ui.R
        x    <- faithful[, 2]
        bins <- input$bins

        # draw the histogram with the specified number of bins
        ggplot2::ggplot(faithful, aes(waiting)) +
          geom_histogram(bins = bins, fill = "darkgray") +
          labs(
            x = 'Waiting time to next eruption (in mins)',
            title = 'Histogram of waiting times'
          ) +
          theme_minimal()
    })
}
```

## Widgets {.smaller}


All share a common setup: 

`widgetInput(inputId, label, ...)`

- `sliderInput` — continuous range
- `radioButtons` — discrete choices, all visible at once
- `selectInput` — dropdown for many choices
- `checkboxGroupInput` — multiple selections

## Change the input {.smaller}

:::{.incremental}
* Let's say instead of a slider, we want 5 options: 
    + 1, 5, 20, 50, or 100
* We can change the input from a slider (representing continuous intervals) <br> to radio buttons, representing discrete choices
* Just swap the widget — `input$bins` still works in the server without any other changes!
* Just change `sliderInput` to `radioButtons`, and put in the appropriate `choices`
    + You can also add a `selected` argument for the default (starting) value
* Be sure to read the `?radioButtons` help documentation!

:::

. . .

Try it out!

`r countdown::countdown(minutes = 5)`

. . .


```{r}
#| eval: false
# Slider (continuous)
sliderInput("bins", "Bins:", min=1, max=50, value=30)

# Radio buttons (discrete)
radioButtons("bins", "Bins:",
             choices = c(1, 5, 20, 50, 100),
             selected = 20)
```

## Try it out!

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|4-7|14"
# Sidebar with a slider input for number of bins
    sidebarLayout(
        sidebarPanel(
          radioButtons("bins",
                        "Number of bins:",
                        choices = list(1, 5, 20, 50, 100),
                        selected = 20
                        )
        )

...

# generate bins based on input$bins from ui.R
        x    <- faithful[, 2]
        bins <- as.numeric(input$bins)
```


## New app (`mpg`)

Use the `mpg` dataset from `{ggplot2}`

* Show distribution of highway mpg (`hwy`) that could be faceted by `year`, `trans`, or `class`
* Keep slider to control bins
* Use `radioButtons` for the variables, and `sliderInput` for the bins
    - You'll need to create a second entry to your `ui` 

. . .

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

## New app (`mpg`) {.smaller}

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)

# Define UI for application that draws a histogram
ui <- fluidPage(

    # Application title
    titlePanel("Highway MPG"),

    # Sidebar with a slider input for number of bins
    sidebarLayout(
        sidebarPanel(
            sliderInput("bins",
                        "Number of bins:",
                        min = 1,
                        max = 50,
                        value = 30),

            radioButtons("facet_var",
                         "Facet by:",
                         choices  = c("Year" = "year",
                                      "Transmission" = "trans",
                                      "Vehicle Class" = "class"),
                         selected = "year")

        ),

        # Show a plot of the generated distribution
        mainPanel(
           plotOutput("distPlot")
        )
    )
)


# Define server logic required to draw a histogram
server <- function(input, output) {

    output$distPlot <- renderPlot({

        ggplot2::ggplot(mpg, aes(hwy)) +
          geom_histogram(bins = input$bins, fill = "darkgray") +
          facet_wrap(vars(!!sym(input$facet_var))) +
          labs(
            x = 'Miles per Gallon (MPG)',
            title = 'Distribution of Highway MPG'
          ) +
          theme_minimal()
    })
}

# Run the application
shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)
```


## More ~~complicated~~ control {.smaller}

* Change the width of the sidebar with <br> `sidebarPanel(width = 2)` 
* Change the height of the main panel plot with <br> `plotOutput("distPlot", height = "800px")` 

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

`?sidebarPanel`

`?plotOutput`

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "9|27"

# Define UI for application that draws a histogram
ui <- fluidPage(

    # Application title
    titlePanel("Highway MPG"),

    # Sidebar with a slider input for number of bins
    sidebarLayout(
        sidebarPanel(width = 2,
            sliderInput("bins",
                        "Number of bins:",
                        min = 1,
                        max = 50,
                        value = 30),

            radioButtons("facet_var",
                         "Facet by:",
                         choices  = c("Year" = "year",
                                      "Transmission" = "trans",
                                      "Vehicle Class" = "class"),
                         selected = "year")

        ),

        # Show a plot of the generated distribution
        mainPanel(
           plotOutput("distPlot", height = "800px")
        )
    )
)

```


## Tables {.smaller}

My recommendation is to use either: 

* `DT::datatable()`
    + sortable, searchable, paginated
* `reactable::reactable()`
    + modern, highly customizable

. . .

Let's build a table to go below our distribution(s) that shows `mean`, `sd`, `min`, and `max` of the corresponding distribution(s)

We'll need to create a new output object in the `server`, and tell the `ui` where it should be rendered

## But first...NSE 🙄 {.smaller}

Working with `{dplyr}` in Shiny

Can't use a column name from `input$var` directly with `{dplyr}`

. . .

This fails

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|3"

output$table <- renderReactable({
  mpg |>
    group_by(input$var) |>
    summarise(
      mean = mean(hwy),
      sd   = sd(hwy),
      min  = min(hwy),
      max  = max(hwy)
    )
})
```


## Why does it fail?

- In non-Shiny `{dplyr}` code, curly-curly `{{}}` passes column names from function arguments
- `{{}}` is for function arguments
- But `input$var` is a `"character"` not an argument so we need a different approach
- `{dplyr}` (with NSE) expects a column name, not a `"character"`

## The Fix {.smaller}

Use `!!sym()` instead of `{{}}`

- `sym()` turns the `class`:`"character"` into the symbol class
- `!!` (bang-bang) tells `{dplyr}` to evaluate it
    + "use the value, not the name"

. . .

This works

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|3"

output$table <- renderReactable({
  mpg |>
    group_by(!!sym(input$var)) |>
    summarise(
      mean = mean(hwy),
      sd   = sd(hwy),
      min  = min(hwy),
      max  = max(hwy)
    )
})
```

## Back to our task

Let's build a table to go below our distribution(s) that shows `mean`, `sd`, `min`, and `max` of the corresponding distribution(s)

We'll need to create a new output object in the `server`, and tell the `ui` where it should be rendered

## Tables {.smaller}

* Use `DT::renderDT` in the server and `DT::DTOutput` in the UI **OR** 
* Use `reactable::renderReactable` in the server and `reactable::reactableOutput` in the UI 
* If you want to use `{dplyr}`, as I would, then you have to deal with NSE
    + In this case it's not that big of a deal
    + Rather than curly-curly `{{}}`
    + Use `!!sym(input$var)` to pass dynamic column names to `{dplyr}`
        - `sym()` transforms a string to a symbol (or name)
        - `!!` evaluates that symbol (bang-bang)


[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

## `mpg` app {.smaller}

::: {.panel-tabset}

## ui

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|27"
ui <- fluidPage(

    # Application title
    titlePanel("Highway MPG"),

    # Sidebar with a slider input for number of bins
    sidebarLayout(
        sidebarPanel(
            sliderInput("bins",
                        "Number of bins:",
                        min = 1,
                        max = 50,
                        value = 30),

            radioButtons("facet_var",
                         "Facet by:",
                         choices  = c("Year" = "year",
                                      "Transmission" = "trans",
                                      "Vehicle Class" = "class"),
                         selected = "year"),
            width = 2
        ),

        # Show a plot of the generated distribution
        mainPanel(
           plotOutput("distPlot"),
           reactableOutput("statsTable")
        )
    )
)
```

## server

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|16-26"
# Define server logic required to draw a histogram
server <- function(input, output) {

    output$distPlot <- renderPlot({

        ggplot2::ggplot(mpg, aes(hwy)) +
          geom_histogram(bins = input$bins, fill = "darkgray") +
          facet_wrap(vars(!!sym(input$facet_var))) +
          labs(
            x = 'Miles per Gallon (MPG)',
            title = 'Distribution of Highway MPG'
          ) +
          theme_minimal()
    })

    output$statsTable <- renderReactable({

      mpg |>
        group_by(!!sym(input$facet_var)) |>
        summarise(
          mean = round(mean(hwy, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
          sd = round(sd(hwy, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
          min = min(hwy, na.rm = TRUE),
          max = max(hwy, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
        reactable()
    })
}
```

:::

# Layouts

Organizing the app's interface

## Layout Options {.smaller}

::: {.incrementa;}
- `sidebarLayout()` — controls on left, output on right
    + most common for simple apps; wrap in `fluidPage()`
- `tabsetPanel()` — tabs within a panel
    + great for separating a plot tab from a table tab
- `navbarPage()` — full navigation bar across the top
    + Each `tabPanel()` is a separate page with its own sidebar
    + Don't share inputId names across pages in `navbarPage()`

:::

## The Bootstrap Grid

Shiny uses a **12-column grid system** under the hood

Every row has 12 units to divide up how you want

- `fluidRow()` + `column()`
    + `fluidRow(column())`
    + fine-grained control
    + `column(width = 4, ...)` = 1/3 of the row

```{r}
#| eval: false

fluidRow(
  column(width = 4, plotOutput("plot1")),   
  column(width = 4, plotOutput("plot2")),   
  column(width = 4, plotOutput("plot3"))    
)
```

## The Bootstrap Grid {.smaller}

Remember

- the number is a share of 12, not a pixel width or a percentage 
- As long as the widths in a `fluidRow()` add up to 12, the columns fill the row evenly
- If they add up to less than 12, the remaining space is just left empty 
    + which is sometimes useful for centering things

| Layout | Code |
|---|---|
| Half / Half | `column(6)` + `column(6)` |
| Sidebar / Main | `column(3)` + `column(9)` |
| Thirds | `column(4)` + `column(4)` + `column(4)` |
| Quarters | `column(3)` + `column(3)` + `column(3)` + `column(3)` |

## Change the layout {.smaller}

Let's say instead of having the plot and table below, we wanted it within a tabset

```{r}
#| eval: false

mainPanel(
           plotOutput("distPlot"),
           reactableOutput("statsTable")
        )
```

. . .

* Just create a `tabsetPanel()` within the `mainPanel()`
    + give it a `title` as the first argument
* Then put the output for each tab within `tabPanel()`

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
mainPanel(
          tabsetPanel(
            tabPanel("Distribution", plotOutput("distPlot")),
            tabPanel("Summary Stats", reactableOutput("statsTable"))
          )
        )
```

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

## Alternative {.smaller}

* You might want to have a navbar at the top of the page, which you can create with `navbarPage()`
* Create each page with `tabPanel()`
* Each `tabPanel` needs to include everything, including (if present) 
    + `sidebarPanel()` 
    + `tabsetPanel()`
    + `mainPanel()`
    + etc.
* Move your title so it's `navbarPage("App title",` or the rest of the pages won't look right
* Make sure **NOT** to share `inputId`'s across pages - it won't work

## `mpg` app {.smaller}

```{r}
#| eval: false

library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)
library(reactable)

ui <- navbarPage("Highway MPG",

    # ── Page 1: Distribution ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    tabPanel("Distribution",
        sidebarLayout(
            sidebarPanel(
                sliderInput("bins",
                            "Number of bins:",
                            min   = 1,
                            max   = 50,
                            value = 30),
                radioButtons("facet_var",
                             "Facet by:",
                             choices  = c("Year"          = "year",
                                          "Transmission"  = "trans",
                                          "Vehicle Class" = "class"),
                             selected = "year"),
                width = 2
            ),
            mainPanel(
                plotOutput("distPlot")
            )
        )
    ),

    # ── Page 2: Summary Stats ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
    tabPanel("Summary Stats",
        sidebarLayout(
            sidebarPanel(
                radioButtons("facet_var_stats",
                             "Group by:",
                             choices  = c("Year"          = "year",
                                          "Transmission"  = "trans",
                                          "Vehicle Class" = "class"),
                             selected = "year"),
                width = 2
            ),
            mainPanel(
                reactableOutput("statsTable")
            )
        )
    )
)

server <- function(input, output) {

    output$distPlot <- renderPlot({
        ggplot(mpg, aes(hwy)) +
            geom_histogram(bins = input$bins, fill = "darkgray") +
            facet_wrap(vars(!!sym(input$facet_var))) +
            labs(
                x     = "Miles per Gallon (MPG)",
                title = "Distribution of Highway MPG"
            ) +
            theme_minimal()
    })

    output$statsTable <- renderReactable({
        mpg |>
            group_by(!!sym(input$facet_var_stats)) |>
            summarise(
                mean = round(mean(hwy, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
                sd   = round(sd(hwy,   na.rm = TRUE), 1),
                min  = min(hwy, na.rm = TRUE),
                max  = max(hwy, na.rm = TRUE)
            ) |>
            reactable()
    })
}

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)
```


## Conclusions

* Shiny is super customizable - and complex!
    + just scratching the surface today
* [Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org) should be your guide for going deeper
* Great for building interactive plots, but you can use it for all sorts of other things too (including text and tables)
* Apps get complicated quickly
    + Consider pulling pieces out as a refactoring method or using [shiny modules](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html)

# Reactivity

How Shiny knows what to update

## Simple example

Try this app. Type the letters in one at a time. Notice how it updates.

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(shiny)

ui <- fluidPage(
  textInput("name", "What's your name?"),
  textOutput("greeting")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$greeting <- renderText({
    paste0("Hello ", input$name, "!")
  })
}
```

::: aside
[Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org/basic-reactivity.html#reactive-programming)
:::

## Programming style

* Notice you don't have to "run" the code each time the input updates
* Your app provides instructions to `R` and Shiny decides when it actually runs the code

. . .

This is known as **declarative programming**

. . .

**Declarative programming** means you provide instructions, but don't actually run it

. . .

Normal `R` code is **imperative programming** - you decide when it's run


## What is reactivity? {.smaller}

::: {incremental}
- Normal `R` is **imperative**
    + you control when code runs
- Shiny uses **declarative** programming
    + you describe relationships, not sequences
    + you describe a dependency graph and Shiny decides when to re-run
    + when `input$bins` changes → anything that depends on it is automatically re-executed
- Think of it like a spreadsheet: change a cell, formulas update automatically

:::

## Key reactive functions

- `render*()`  — creates a reactive output
- `reactive()` — creates a reusable reactive value/expression
- `observe()`  — runs side effects (e.g., writing to a file)
- `observeEvent()` — runs when a specific input changes

## Reactivity

- Shiny builds a graph of dependencies at runtime
- input → reactive expression → output
- When an input changes, only the affected outputs re-execute
- You can visualize this with the [`{reactlog}`]((https://rstudio.github.io/reactlog/)) package

## Inputs 

`input` is a basically a list object that contains objects from the ui

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
ui <- fluidPage(
  numericInput("count", label = "Number of values", value = 100)
)
```

After writing this code, `input$count` will be a available in the server, and the value it takes will depend on the browser input (starting at 100)

These are read-only, and cannot be modified

## Selective read permissions

It must be in a reactive context, or it won't work

That's why this results in an error - trying to `print()` in the server won't work outside a reactive context

```{r}
#| eval: false
server <- function(input, output, session) {
  print(paste0("The value of input$count is ", input$count)) 
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
# > Error in .getReactiveEnvironment()$currentContext() : 
# >  Operation not allowed without an active reactive context. 
# > (You tried to do something that can only be done from inside a reactive expression or observer.)
```

## Output

* The `output` object is similar to `input`, in terms of being a list-like object
* Create new components of the list for new output, and refer to them in the `ui`
* These also need to be in reactive contexts (e.g., `render*()`)

## `reactive()` tips {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

- If two outputs need the same data, don't repeat yourself
- Wrap shared computation in `reactive()` — it's cached and only re-runs when inputs change
- Call it like a function (with parentheses)
    + `filtered_data()`
- But never call reactive expressions outside a reactive context

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|4-6|9,13"
server <- function(input, output, session) {

  # Shared reactive expression — runs once, used by both outputs
  filtered_data <- reactive({
    mpg |> filter(class == !!sym(input$class))
  })

  output$plot  <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(filtered_data(), aes(displ, hwy)) + geom_point()
  })

  output$table <- renderTable({
    filtered_data()
  })
}
```

:::

::::

## Why reactivity?

Imagine we want to have a simple app converting temperatures from Fahrenheit to Celsius

. . .

Do this with variables

```{r }
temp_f <- 72
temp_c <- (temp_f - 32) * (5/9)
temp_c
```

. . .

But changing `temp_f` has no impact on `temp_c`

```{r }
temp_f <- 50
temp_c
```

## Use a function?

Let's instead make this a function that depends on the object in the global environment.

```{r }
to_celsius <- function() {
  (temp_f - 32) * (5/9)
}
to_celsius()
temp_f <- 30
to_celsius()
```

. . .

This works, but it's less than ideal computationally. 

Even if `temp_f` hasn't changed, the conversion is re-computed

Not a big deal in this case, but often is.

## Reactive alternative

First create a reactive variable

```{r }
library(shiny)
reactiveConsole(TRUE)
temp_f <- reactiveVal(72)
temp_f()
temp_f(50)
temp_f()
```

## Reactive function

Next create a reactive function

```{r }
to_celsius <- reactive({
  message("Converting...")
  (temp_f() - 32) * (5/9)
})
```

. . .

Now it will convert **only** when the value of `temp_f()` changes

## Reactive function

Unless the value of `temp_f` changes, the code will not be re-run.

```{r}
#| message: true
to_celsius()
```

. . .

```{r}
#| message: true
to_celsius()
```

. . .

```{r}
#| message: true
temp_f(100)
to_celsius()
```

. . .

```{r}
#| message: true
to_celsius()
```

. . .

```{r}
#| message: true
temp_f(75)
to_celsius()
```

## Reactive graph {visibility="hidden"}

* Normally, you understand `R` code by running it top to bottom
* This doesn't work with shiny
* Instead, we think through reactive graphs

![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/056990a803a41501e2dbe05df351f8b8f7b493ab/f773a/diagrams/basic-reactivity/graph-1b.png)

::: aside
[Mastering Shiny](https://mastering-shiny.org/basic-reactivity.html#the-reactive-graph)
:::

## [reactlog](https://rstudio.github.io/reactlog/) {visibility="hidden"}

![](http://rstudio.github.io/reactlog/readme-images/pythagoras.gif)

## Basic example {visibility="hidden"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(shiny)
library(reactlog)

# Enable logging BEFORE running the app
reactlog_enable()

ui <- fluidPage(
  textInput("name", "What's your name?"),
  textOutput("greeting")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$greeting <- renderText({
    paste0("Hello ", input$name, "!")
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

# After closing the app, view the reactive log:
reactlogShow()
```

# Deployment

Deploy your Shiny App

## Deployment {.smaller}

Once built, you will need to deploy your Shiny app on the web

Two (potentially free) options:

::: {.incremental}
1) [shinyapps.io](https://www.shinyapps.io/)
    - 👍 been using it for years
    - 👎 ending in 2026, migrating to
2) posit [Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/)
    - 👍 next-gen publishing platform
    - 👍 more capabilities and better security
    
:::

## Deployment {.smaller}

Once built, you will need to deploy your Shiny app on the web

One ~~Two~~ (potentially free) option~~s~~:

1) ~~[shinyapps.io](https://www.shinyapps.io/)~~
    - ~~👍 been using it for years~~
    - ~~👎 ending in 2026, migrating to~~
2) posit [Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/)
    - 👍 next-gen publishing platform
    - 👍 more capabilities and better security

Posit [Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/) it is!

## Sign up

Go to <https://connect.posit.cloud/>

Sign up for a free account

- Note that it will ask you to choose your account name
- This name will appear in your published URLs
    + Shiny app or Quarto
- So **make sure** it is professional!

## Deploy to Connect Cloud

1) Make sure your RStudio Project is connected to Github
2) Build your Shiny app
3) Preview the app
4) Add dependency file
5) Push to Github
6) Deploy to Posit Connect Cloud
7) Republish the app (as needed)

::: aside
[Tutorial](https://docs.posit.co/connect-cloud/how-to/r/shiny-r.html)
:::

## Deploy to Connect Cloud {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
1) Make sure your RStudio Project is connected to Github
2) Build your Shiny app
3) Preview the app
    + In RStudio when looking at `app.R`, click the `Run` button
    + This previews your application locally
4) Add dependency file
    + Create a dependency file so Connect Cloud can rebuild your content during deployment
    + Load the `{rsconnect}` package and run `writeManifest()`

:::

. . .

```{r}
#| eval: false
rsconnect::writeManifest()
```

## Deploy to Connect Cloud

After you `writeManifest()`

5) Push to Github

## Deploy to Connect Cloud {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
6) Deploy to Posit Connect Cloud
    1. Sign in to Connect Cloud
    2. Click the "*Publish*" icon button on the top of your Home page
    3. Select Shiny
    4. Select the public repository that you created
    5. Confirm the branch
    6. Select `app.R` as the primary file
    7. Click "*Publish*"
        + will display status updates during the deployment process
        + also build logs will stream on the lower part of the screen
    8. 🎉 Share the link!

:::

## Deploy to Connect Cloud

7) Republish the app (as needed)

. . .

If you update your app code or the data

1. Commit and push the changes to your GitHub repository
2. Republish the app on Connect Cloud
    i. Go to your Content List
    ii. Clicking the "*republish*" icon


## Conclusions

* Shiny is super customizable - almost limitless (see more examples [here](https://shiny.posit.co/r/gallery/))
* Really helpful and fun way to build data tools for practitioners
* Takes some practice, but basically allows you to write normal `R` code, and get interactive websites

# Next time

## Before next class

**No class next week!**

Before Week 10

- Readings
    + [Mastering Shiny: Ch3](https://mastering-shiny.org/basic-reactivity.html)
    + [Mastering Shiny: Ch5](https://mastering-shiny.org/basic-reactivity.html)
    + [Mastering Shiny: Ch17](https://mastering-shiny.org/best-practices.html)
